course_title: Tort Claims Against Government - Private Remedies for Public Wrongs

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate detailed knowledge of the principles governing the tortious liability of public authorities for malicious prosecution, misfeasance in public office, false imprisonment and negligence (with a particular focus on the negligence liability of the police, child-protection agencies, prison and health authorities and local councils).
Demonstrate advanced problem-solving skills within the field.
Demonstrate advanced, independent research-skills within the field, including the ability to locate and reference materials beyond those provided, through use of libraries and on-line databases.
Demonstrate ability to communicate ideas and research findings effectively in writing and to observe disciplinary citation practices.

assessments.task_description: This written problem-solving exercise will span the course syllabus, testing your understanding of the various topics and your ability to research and apply the relevant principles to a novel set of facts. It involves the conduct of independent research of the law, as well as the application of that law to advise a client about a concrete set of facts.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Detailed submission guidelines will be provided via blackboard closer to the submission date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You will be examined in a closed book format on two research essay topics chosen from a list of pre-defined topics made available in advance of the examination.

The topics are to be researched in advance, using the course materials as a starting point but then also referring to further materials and arguments gathered independently through the use of library and on-line research resources databases. Essays in the examination will distil and present considered research findings on the topics in question under secure conditions.

The purpose of this format is to enable students to engage in depth with material that is of particular interest to them in order to develop higher levels of insight and understanding than is customarily possible in a closed book examination format.

A total of two essays must be completed during the examination.

Each essay carries equal marks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 12(27 Jul - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

2 hours seminars run weekly from the week beginning 27/7 to the week beginning 19/10 (inclusive). The first hour normally involves general discussion of the week's readings, led by the course co-ordinator or another guest expert. The second involves student activities, such as team problem-solving, presentations, or quizzes. There are no lectures in this subject and students are required to complete the relevant readings in advance of each seminar.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
